```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).

% For each client, assign exactly one masseuse and one price.
{assign(Client, Masseuse, Price): masseuse(Masseuse), price(Price)}=1 :- clients(Client).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
vintage(1984; 1988; 1992; 1996).
wine("Annata Branco"; "Friambliss"; "Luzagueil"; "Zifennwein").
type("chardonnay"; "merlot"; "pinot gris"; "syrah").

% Define the assignment predicate: assign(W, V, T) means wine W has vintage V and type T.
assign(W, V, T) :- wine(W), vintage(V), type(T).

% Clue 1: The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
assign("Luzagueil", _, "chardonnay").

% Clue 2: The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
assign("Annata Branco", "1992", _).
assign("Annata Branco", _, "syrah").

% Clue 3: The Friambliss is a syrah.
assign("Friambliss", _, "syrah").

% Clue 4: Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
% Case 1: pinot gris → Luzagueil, 1984 → Zifennwein
assign("Luzagueil", _, "pinot gris") :- assign("Zifennwein", "1984", _).
% Case 2: pinot gris → Zifennwein, 1984 → Luzagueil
assign("Zifennwein", _, "pinot gris") :- assign("Luzagueil", "1984", _).

% Clue 5: The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.
% So: vintage(pinot gris) = vintage(merlot) + 4
:- assign(_, V_p, "pinot gris"), assign(_, V_m, "merlot"), V_p != V_m + 4.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
islands("Fushil"; "Jujihm"; "Nuhirk"; "Verinya").
years(1754; 1761; 1768; 1775).
cultures("Hakili"; "Manika'i"; "Kukani"; "Wainani").

% Define the relation: discover(I, Y, C) means island I was discovered in year Y and had culture C.
% Each island is matched to exactly one year and one culture.
{discover(I, Y, C) : years(Y), cultures(C)}=1 :- islands(I).
{discover(I, Y, C) : islands(I), cultures(C)}=1 :- years(Y).
{discover(I, Y, C) : islands(I), years(Y)}=1 :- cultures(C).

% Clue 1: Jujihm was discovered in 1768.
:- not discover("Jujihm", 1768, _).

% Clue 2: Verinya was discovered in 1761.
:- not discover("Verinya", 1761, _).

% Clue 3: Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So: year of Jujihm = year of Wainani + 14 → 1768 = Y + 14 → Y = 1754.
:- not discover(_, 1754, "Wainani").

% Clue 4: The island on which the Kukani people lived was discovered 7 years after Fushil.
% So: year(Kukani) = year(Fushil) + 7.
% We encode this by ensuring that if Fushil is discovered in Y, then Kukani is discovered in Y+7.
% We use a constraint to enforce this.
:- discover("Fushil", Y, _), not discover(_, Y+7, "Kukani").

% Clue 5: The island discovered in 1768 was either the island on which the Hakili people lived or Fushil.
% But Jujihm is the only island discovered in 1768, and Jujihm ≠ Fushil, so Jujihm must have Hakili culture.
:- not discover("Jujihm", 1768, "Hakili").
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
craters("Asanish"; "Cersay"; "Garight"; "Kimeta").
diameter(100; 125; 150; 175).
period("Cambrian"; "Devonian"; "Jurassic"; "Ordovician").

% Define the single predicate to relate crater, diameter, and period.
crater_info(C, D, P) :- craters(C), diameter(D), period(P).

% Each crater is assigned exactly one diameter and one period.
{crater_info(C, D, P) : diameter(D), period(P)} = 1 :- craters(C).

% Clue 1: The Garight crater is 150 meters wide.
:- not crater_info("Garight", 150, _).

% Clue 2: The Cersay crater was formed during the Cambrian period.
:- not crater_info("Cersay", _, "Cambrian").

% Clue 3: The Cersay crater is 50 meters wider than the Jurassic crater.
% Let D1 be diameter of Cersay, D2 be diameter of Jurassic crater.
% Then D1 = D2 + 50.
:- crater_info("Cersay", D1, _), crater_info("Jurassic", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 50.

% Clue 4: The Asanish crater is 25 meters smaller than the Ordovician crater.
% Let D1 be diameter of Asanish, D2 be diameter of Ordovician crater.
% Then D1 = D2 - 25.
:- crater_info("Asanish", D1, _), crater_info("Ordovician", D2, _), D1 != D2 - 25.
```

% Define the constants in each category.

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
height(144; 147; 150; 153).
tree("Evan's Fir"; "Old Jarvis"; "Nolan's Pine"; "Zeke's Spruce").
age(79; 80; 96; 99).

% Define tree_info(T, H, A) to represent a tree T with height H and age A.
% Each tree has exactly one height and one age.
{tree_info(T, H, A) : height(H), age(A)}=1 :- tree(T).

% Constraint: The 147-ft tree is either "Nolan's Pine" or "Zeke's Spruce"
:- tree_info(T, 147, A), T != "Nolan's Pine", T != "Zeke's Spruce".

% Constraint: The 80-year-old tree is either "Nolan's Pine" or "Zeke's Spruce"
:- tree_info(T, H, 80), T != "Nolan's Pine", T != "Zeke's Spruce".

% Constraint: The 147-ft tree and the 80-year-old tree are different trees
:- tree_info("Nolan's Pine", 147, _), tree_info("Nolan's Pine", _, 80).
:- tree_info("Zeke's Spruce", 147, _), tree_info("Zeke's Spruce", _, 80).

% The 147-ft tree and the 80-year-old tree must be exactly "Nolan's Pine" and "Zeke's Spruce" in some order
% This is enforced by the above constraints and the fact that only these two trees satisfy both conditions.
% We use the following to ensure that one of them is 147-ft and the other is 80-year-old.
% The model will generate only solutions where one tree is 147-ft and the other is 80-year-old, and they are different.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
owners("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% Core predicate: win(Owner, Year, Breed) - each dog is uniquely identified by owner, year, and breed.
win(E, Y, B) :- owners(E), year(Y), breed(B).

% Each owner has exactly one dog (one win per owner).
{win(E, Y, B) : year(Y), breed(B)} = 1 :- owners(E).

% Each year has exactly one dog.
{win(E, Y, B) : owners(E), breed(B)} = 1 :- year(Y).

% Each breed is used exactly once.
{win(E, Y, B) : owners(E), year(Y)} = 1 :- breed(B).

% Clue 3: Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
:- not win("Ginger", Y, "bulldog") : year(Y).

% Clue 1: The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
:- not (exists Y1, Y2, E, B : win("Fernando", Y1, B), win(E, Y2, "dalmatian"), Y2 = Y1 + 1).

% Clue 2: Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
:- not (exists Y1, Y2, E : win("Ginger", Y1, B), win(E, Y2, "irish setter"), Y2 = Y1 + 1).

% Clue 4: The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog, and Elsie's dog.
% This implies that the set of dogs is exactly these four, so we can express it via uniqueness and coverage.
% Since we already enforce one dog per owner, year, and breed, and there are four of each,
% the only way this clue adds value is to ensure that the 2006 winner, dalmatian, bulldog, and Elsie's dog are all distinct and cover all four.
% But since all assignments are bijective, this is automatically satisfied if we have one of each.
% However, to enforce that Elsie's dog is one of the four, and the 2006 winner is one, etc.,
% we can use the following: the dalmatian and bulldog are different, and Elsie's dog is not necessarily the 2006 winner,
% but the clue says the four dogs are exactly these four — so we can use this to eliminate any solution where,
% for example, Elsie's dog is not among the 2006 winner, dalmatian, bulldog, or the irish setter (but that’s not directly stated).

% Actually, the clue says: "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog, and Elsie's dog."
% So the four entities are:
% 1. The 2006 winner
% 2. The dalmatian
% 3. The bulldog
% 4. Elsie's dog
% And these are the only four dogs.

% Therefore, every dog must be one of these four.

% But since there are exactly four dogs, and we have one per owner, we can express this by noting that:
% - The dalmatian must be one of the four
% - The bulldog must be one of the four
% - Elsie's dog must be one of the four
% - The 2006 winner must be one of the four

% But since all dogs are covered by the bijective constraints, we can use the following:
% The set of dogs is exactly the set of:
%   - (Y=2006, any E, B)
%   - (B=dalmatian, any E, Y)
%   - (B=bulldog, any E, Y)
%   - (E=Elsie, any Y, B)

% So every dog must satisfy at least one of these.

% But that’s hard to express directly. Instead, we can use the fact that the dalmatian and bulldog are distinct,
% and Elsie's dog is not necessarily the 2006 winner, but all four are distinct.

% However, the key insight is that the clue is redundant under the bijective constraints — unless it forces a specific assignment.

% But to be safe, we can add:

:- win(E, Y, B), not (Y = 2006; B = "dalmatian"; B = "bulldog"; E = "Elsie").

% But this is not quite right: it says "the dog that won in

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee(1; 2; 3; 4).
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% The primary relation: each employee is assigned to one section and one rider count.
assign(E, S, R) :- employee(E), section(S), riders(R).

% Each employee is assigned to exactly one section and one rider count.
{assign(E, S, R) : section(S), riders(R)} = 1 :- employee(E).

% Each section is assigned to exactly one employee.
{assign(E, S, R) : employee(E), riders(R)} = 1 :- section(S).

% Each rider count is assigned to exactly one employee.
{assign(E, S, R) : employee(E), section(S)} = 1 :- riders(R).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% For each player, they play in exactly one position and have exactly one number of games.
{plays(Player, Position, Games): positions(Position), games(Games)}=1 :- players(Player).
```
